slides.title: The four pillars of OOP
body: Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Abstraction. Every OOP language since Smalltalk is built on them. Apex inherits these from Java.
Real example: Salesforce's own SObject class hierarchy IS these pillars in action — every Account, Contact, Opportunity is a class extending SObject.

vocabulary.def: Object-Oriented Programming — paradigm built on classes, instances, and the four pillars.

slides.title: Encapsulation
body: Hide internals. Expose a clean public interface; keep guts private. We covered this in the Methods & Properties lessons (private fields, public methods).
Real example: Stripe's Charge class — public methods like create() and refund(); private internals you never see.

vocabulary.def: Bundling data and behavior into a class while hiding internal details from outside callers.

slides.title: Inheritance — extends
body: A child class can EXTEND a parent. It inherits the parent's properties and methods, can add its own, and can OVERRIDE the parent's behavior.
Real example: imagine a generic Vehicle class with drive() and fuel(). Car extends Vehicle. Truck extends Vehicle. Both inherit drive(); each has its own behavior.

vocabulary.def: Apex keyword: 'class B extends A' means B inherits A's members.
Modifier on a parent method or class meaning 'subclasses are allowed to override me.'

slides.title: Parent class with virtual method
slides.intro: Type this. 'virtual' lets subclasses override drive().
lineExplanations: 'public virtual class' — virtual on the class lets it be subclassed.
Property anyone can read/write.
'public virtual void drive' — virtual on the method lets subclasses override it.
Default behavior.
Method and class close.

slides.title: Child class with override
slides.intro: Type this. Truck extends Vehicle and overrides drive().
lineExplanations: Truck extends Vehicle — Truck inherits 'name' and the virtual drive().
'override' on the method — explicit signal that we're replacing the parent's behavior.
New body. Note we still have access to inherited 'name' from Vehicle.
Method and class close.

slides.title: Polymorphism
body: ONE variable typed as the parent can hold ANY child instance and the right method runs based on the actual type. 'Many shapes', one interface.
Real example: a List<Vehicle> can hold Cars, Trucks, Motorcycles. Looping it and calling .drive() runs each one's specific implementation.

vocabulary.def: One variable type can hold many concrete subclass instances; the right method runs based on actual type.

slides.title: Polymorphism in action
slides.intro: Type this. The same .drive() call dispatches to different code per vehicle.
lineExplanations: List typed as parent (Vehicle) — can hold any subclass.
Add a plain Vehicle.
Add a Truck — fits in a Vehicle list because Truck IS A Vehicle.
Loop over the parent type.
Calling v.drive() runs Vehicle's drive on the Vehicle instance, and Truck's overridden drive on the Truck instance.

slides.title: Abstraction — abstract classes
body: abstract class can declare methods WITHOUT a body. Subclasses MUST implement them. Useful for 'this thing must exist but I don't know how each subclass does it'.
Real example: a Notification class has abstract send(). EmailNotification extends it and implements send() for SMTP. SlackNotification implements send() differently. Both ARE Notifications.

vocabulary.def: Modifier — abstract class can't be instantiated directly; abstract method has no body and must be overridden.

slides.title: Abstract class
slides.intro: Type this. Notification declares send() as abstract — subclasses are forced to implement it.
lineExplanations: abstract class — cannot be 'new Notification()' directly.
abstract method — no body, just a signature. Must be overridden by subclasses.
Concrete (non-abstract) method — subclasses inherit this for free.
Body of log.
Methods and class close.

slides.title: Interfaces — pure contracts
body: An interface is a CONTRACT — only method signatures, no bodies, no fields. A class 'implements' the interface and provides bodies.
Real example: Apex's own Queueable interface — implement execute() however you want; the platform calls it. You and Salesforce never need to touch each other's source.

vocabulary.def: A pure contract — only method signatures. Classes implement it by providing bodies.
Apex keyword: 'class A implements B' means A promises to provide all methods B declares.

slides.title: Defining an interface
slides.intro: Type this. Pure contract — no bodies.
lineExplanations: Interface declaration. No constructors, no fields.
Method signature only — semicolon, no body.
Another. Classes implementing Sender must provide BOTH methods.
Close.

slides.title: Implementing the interface
slides.intro: Type this. EmailSender implements Sender — must provide send() and isReady().
lineExplanations: Class implements Sender — promises to provide all of its methods.
Provide send() with a real body.
Body — actual email logic would go here.
Close.
Provide isReady() with a real body.
Real-world might check API connectivity.
Close.

slides.title: Implementing multiple interfaces
slides.intro: A class can implement more than one interface at once — comma-separated. The class then must provide method bodies for ALL methods declared by all the interfaces. Common pattern in async Apex: a class that's both Schedulable AND Database.Batchable.
lineExplanations: Class implements TWO interfaces — comma-separated. Now the class must satisfy both contracts.
Schedulable.execute — runs once when the cron fires.
Body — kick off the batch by passing 'this' (the same class instance is also Batchable).
Close.
Batchable.start — returns the QueryLocator that defines the chunkable result set.
Inline SOQL wrapped in a QueryLocator.
Close.
Batchable.execute — note this is a SECOND execute method, but with a different signature (Apex allows overloading by signature).
Close — body would process the chunk.
Batchable.finish — runs once after all chunks complete.
Close.
Close the class.

slides.front: What's the difference between an abstract class and an interface?
slides.back: Abstract class CAN have concrete methods and fields; interface has ONLY method signatures.
slides.detail: Use abstract class when subclasses share real implementation. Use interface when you just want to enforce a contract — multiple classes can implement many interfaces, but extend only one class.

slides.question: Apex's Queueable — which OOP construct?
options: An interface — you implement execute() yourself.
A concrete class with virtual methods.
slides.explanation: Queueable is an interface. Your class implements Queueable, provides execute(), and the platform calls it on schedule.

slides.title: Capstone: Notification interface (Email + Slack)
slides.intro: Your platform needs to send notifications via either email OR Slack. Use an interface so the same calling code works for both — the textbook strategy pattern, which is exactly how Salesforce's own framework code stays swappable for different integrations.
lineExplanations: Define the contract — interfaces have only signatures, no bodies.
Every implementer must provide this method.
Close the interface.
EmailSender promises to satisfy the Sender contract.
Provide the send method — same signature as the interface declared.
EmailSender's actual logic. Real code would call SingleEmailMessage.
Close send.
Close EmailSender.
SlackSender — same contract, different impl.
Same signature.
Slack's logic. Real code would HTTP POST to a webhook.
Close send.
Close SlackSender.
Polymorphism in action: a List of Senders can hold any implementer.
Walk the list — calling code doesn't know or care which class it's holding.
Each .send() dispatches to the actual class's implementation. Email runs Email's, Slack runs Slack's.
Close the loop.

slides.title: What you now know
body: Encapsulation, inheritance (extends + virtual + override), polymorphism (parent type holding subclass), abstraction (abstract classes), and interfaces.
Next: Async Apex — push work to the background when synchronous limits aren't enough.
